Thesis notes

Focus on methodology. Highlight sampling

Code:

Bost et al. 2023: Only randomly picked, square vTMAs. R.

Forjaz et al. 2024: Preprint, no code available

Khan & Yuan 2016: No code available

Lee et al. 2019: No code available

Lin et al. 2023 method of vTMA assembly unclear. MCMICRO or custom scripts? Text mentions custom python scripts but Github is all Matlab.

Liudahl et al. 2021: No code available.

Mi et al. 2025: Mix of ipynb, R, and Matlab. Largely undocumented.

Oshiro et al. 2016: No code available.

Sobottka et al. 2024: No code available

Weis et al. 2018: Pseudocode and Matlab available

Eskaros et al. 2017: No code available

Table of content

Paper summaries:

Azzalini et al. 2023:

Overview of Tumor Heterogeneity in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancers

Overview of HGSOC tumor heterogeneity. HGSOC are 70% of epithelial ovarian carcinomas (EOC). Typically found in advanced stages. Characterized by remarkable intratumor heterogeneity, with different morphologies and tissue structures within the same tumor. 96% have TP53 mutations, many of which also have high chromosomal instability (CIN) and homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), which is a biomarker for platinum and PARP-inhibitor therapy. Disease progression, resistance and recurrence are linked to ITH in tumor masses.

H&E morphologies classified into transitional, solid, pseudo-endometroid, papillary, infiltrative micropapillae, and papillary infiltrative, divided into classic papillary and SET groups. Molecular morphologies classified into mesenchymal transition (MT), immune reactive (IR), solid and proliferative (SP), and papilloglandular (PG) types. IR and PG types have better outcomes than MT and SP. Typically multiple morphologies present in one sample. Literature inaccurate on reported frequencies as often only the prevalent morphology in a sample is considered. Papillary features most widespread. Some morphologies mimic other EOC types. BRCA mutations associated with SET characteristics, high levels of mitotic index and TIL, serous “undifferentiated” morphology, and giant atypical nuclei, along with different metastatic patterns. SET features have better response and outcome over classical features. Heterogeneity of tumor growth patterns measured by Shannon’s index is a prognostic biomarker under neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

Morphologic features in WSI can predict molecular alterations like BRCA deficiency and MSI-H, as well as prognosis. Positive and negative morphologies can be present in the same sample. TME interactions can be behind heterogeneity. Stromal cells seen as a resource for tumor growth. Shift towards resource-rich TME associated with aggressive cancer behavior. Insufficient evidence to link IHC biomarkers and growth patterns. Morphological and clonal heterogeneity don’t always overlap. Only occasional spatial correspondence between IHC biomarkers and tumor pattern.

Histo-morphologies are correlated with varying transcriptional signatures, which predict prognosis. HM also correlated with pre-operative features, such as MT type more common in late-stage peritoneal tumors with worse progn. Different HM types related to different sets of genes. Association between HGSOC architecture and molecular and biophysical features, such as tumor stiffness and AKT isoform expression pattern, has been shown. Expression patterns from bulk tissue analysis can be heavily influenced by stromal and immune cells. It’s possible that MT HM mirrors stromal cell expression, while IR tumors are SP or PC tumors with a prominent immune response. Temporal and spatial evolution changes morphology. Three temporal trajectories have been identified: evolving (early), maintaining (intermediate), and adapting (late), each with different typical cell content, genomic and transcriptional features.

Tumors have been classified with preop laparoscopic imaging into two types: (1) deep and invasive appearance with distorted or retracted surrounding tissues, or (2) superficial appearance with exophytic nodules outlined by normal tissue. Both have distinct surgical outcomes and molecular signatures. Histological analysis shows no significant microscopy pattern in the types, although papillary architecture is more common in type 1 (50% vs. 5%). The types also have distinct radiographic features.

Clonal evolution is related to genomic instability as a major feature of the carcinogenic process. HGSOC is genomically unstable, with the underlying biological processes being established early. The genomic variations of each patient are very heterogeneous. p53 loss near ubiquitous, sold tumors 100% but ascites 91% CIN, suggesting a dynamic process. Extensive HRD and CNV driving ITH through dysregulation of apoptosis and DNA repair. Point mutations at tumor suppressors or oncogenes are unusual. Distinct mutational profiles associated with age at diagnosis. Tumor suppressor loss and oncogene amplification mainly due to loss of heterozygosity (LOH), telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI), and large-scale state transitions. PTEN, RB1, NF1, RAD51B breakage are common in HGSOC. CCNE1, MYC, MECOM, and PIK3CA often amplified.

Quantifying ITH in HGSOC is difficult because it requires inferring phylogenetic trees with unknown parental allele phasing and horizontal dependencies between adjacent genomic loci. Studies of ITH in HGSOC have been focused on spatial and temporal ITH by comparing primary tumors to metastases. No studies multisampling primary tumors, lack of information on the possible mono-/polyclonal origin of the disease. Currently, it’s feasible to consider tumors polyclonal. Spatial ITH likely arises from metastasis to metastasis spread, rather than successive metastases from primary tumor. Clonal evolution from primary cancer to recurrences has been documented spatio-temporally. High clonal expansion associated with drug resistant clones.

Two evolutionary topologies proposed: star (polyclonal origin, monoclonal spreading), and tree (monoclonal origin, polyclonal spreading). Heterogeneity driven by evolutionary moves increasing genomic instability and subclonal expansion. ITH calls for multi-site biopsies.

Epigenetic regulation is prevalent in clonal evolution of HGSOC. HRD can result from methylation of BRCA promoters. Epigenetic HRD has worse prognosis and Pt-resistance than genetic HRD, comparable to HR type cancers. Targeting hypermethylation could be a treatment opportunity. On the other hand, genome-wide hypomethylation is also associated with cancer features, such as CIN, depression of imprinted genes and retrotransposons, and aberrant expression. There is near universal CpG hypomethylation in HGSOC, Hypomethylation at three distinct chromosomal sites categorises patients with better prognoses over hypermethylated HGSOC. Histone modification can be involved in tumorigenesis and drug resistance, but in the case of H3K14ac being associated with PARPi resistance, its medical depletion didn’t re-sensitize the cancer cells, which suggests that other histone modification -associated proteins are directly involved in the resistance. Upregulation of other histone modifications and involved proteins are also associated with resistance.

Paired adnexal and omentum samples highlight non-clonal ITH by different classifications at each site with a systematic shift towards mesenchymal type at extra-adnexal sites. MT-heavy tumors are characterized by low genomic alteration, activation of EM-transitional TFs, decreased epithelial markers, increased mesenchymal markers, and diverse cell composition. The EMT index is a measure of these features, and has prognostic value. Mesenchymal tumors are associated with Pt-resistance and poor prognosis. It has been proposed that differentiated tumors represent an early stage in tumor evolution, while proliferative tumors are a later state.

Non-genetic evolution in HGSOC drives a shift towards high metabolism and proliferation with decreasing immune response as multi-resistance emerges. Inter- and intravariability in both malignant and non-malignant cells, indicating different functional subpopulations shaping the ecosystem. The dynamics between tumor and TME have variability associated with poor or excellent neoadjuvant chemotherapy response. Stronger responders have more immune-related tumor areas, while weak responders have more mesenchymal-derived tumor areas.

Immune cold HGSOC, which are the majority, have been linked to CNVs in chr 4q, proliferative subtype and proficient HR. Immune hot tumors are less frequent and have high mutational burden and HRD, with better prognosis. These types affect response to immune checkpoint inhibitors. CD3 expression is decremented in higher cancer stages likely due to induction of immune checkpoint inhibition and T-cell exhaustion. T-cell TME is markedly different in ovarian tumors compared to omental metastases, with immunesuppression by T-regs and exhausted CD4+ and CD8+ T cells in ovary and non-tumor-specific bystander TILs in omentum. Immune cell composition also varies across adnexal, non-adnexal and ascitic sites as a result of different mutational processes, suggesting that mechanisms of immune resistance aren’t universal in a patient. There is also spatial variation in T-cell infiltration intra-lesion, greater than observed inter-site or inter-patient. Chemotherapy can induce local immune response in immune cold tumors.

CAF induce the EMT process. Intra- and interstromal heterogeneity is documented by spatial transcriptomics, stratifying long and short survival, density of CAF in stromal clusters, and their location relative to the tumor. Long survivors had more TIL, stromal cells and myofibrils in their TME. Short survivors had less TIL and more CAFs expressing POSTN near the tumor-stroma interface. Ligand-receptor analysis shows that tumor-derived LRP5 and CAF derived APOE are associated with a more aggressive disease.

TME has a dynamic and evolving ECM composition, with progression promoting or suppressing cell interactions. Macrophages seem to promote immunesuppression in HGSOC precursors, and tumor growth in vitro. To best understand HGSOC, both the tumor and TME should be analyzed as they both contribute to tumor progression and resistance.

Progress in HGSOC has had limited translatability to the clinic. PARPi, Bevacizumab, and HRD testing are in use, but HRD testing is imperfect in predicting PARPi response. Pt-therapy response is still the litmus-test. PARPi has increased the survival of mainly genomic HRD patients.Surgical specimen should be characterized comprehensively, examining the architecture in both primary and metastatic sites through multisampling, to distinguish between SET and classic morphologies. Also IHC characterization of immune and stromal cells is crucial to identify potential immunotherapy responders.

Besusparis et al. 2016

Impact of tissue sampling on accuracy of Ki67 immunohistochemistry evaluation in breast cancer

vTMA. Model of TMA sampling in breast cancer for Ki-67. Tumors can be divided into a homogeneous and a heterogenous subset. To achieve a Ki-67 LI (Ki67 labeling index) coefficient of error <10%, homogenous tumors need 5-6 cores, heterogeneous tumors 11-12 cores, and mixed cases 8 cores. Core diameter 0.75 mm. Large SD. Random sampling from an invasive tumor area.

Approach:

“TMA simulation using hexagonal tiling”

The hexagons in the HexT (hexagonal tiling) were chosen to simulate virtual TMA cores (or corresponding fields of view in conventional microscopy), with numbers of Ki67 positive and negative cells established by DIA (digital image analysis). From this, intratumoral Ki67 heterogeneity indicators (Haralick’s entropy values) were extracted and used to dichotomize the tumors into homogeneous and heterogeneous subsets.

Simulations with random selection of hexagons, equivalent to 0.75 mm circular diameter TMA cores, were performed. The tissue sampling requirements were investigated in relation to tumor heterogeneity using linear regression and extended error analysis.

Hexagons containing no nuclear profiles by DIA were regarded as missing data; hexagons containing fewer than 100 nuclear profiles were regarded as insufficiently sampled.

Each subset is sampled from the set of hexagonal tiles without replacement, but all hexagons are replaced before sampling a new subset.

Two approaches were used:

First, virtual TMA construction, in which a set of cores is sampled only once, by randomly sampling a subset of hexagons. Single linear regression analysis was used to compare the data in a single random selection.

Second, Coefficient error formula (T is the ground truth in the WSI, μ is mean of Ki67 LI inferred from the simulation ):

Bost et al. 2023

Optimizing multiplexed imaging experimental design through tissue spatial segregation estimation

vTMA. TMA vs WSI. Model of cell types present and their proportions as a function of sampling parameters. Spatial correlation. Translation of spatial transcriptomic results to IHC. Multiple tissue types with variable spatial segregation. TMA sampling strategies wrt. core number and size. Higher core number beneficial for highly segregated tissues. KL-divergence as a measure of error. Random TMA sampling.

Approach:

“Modeling TMA construction by sampling square areas from Visium -spatial transcriptomic arrays”

The study clustered Visium data to represent distinct cell types’ spatial segregation in a tissue map, and then used random sampling of non-overlapping square fields of vision (FoV) of various sizes to simulate TMA construction on it. The amount of clusters recovered by the sampling represents the number of unique cell phenotypes discovered by the “TMA”. They derived a model for the number of clusters recovered as a function of number of FoVs sampled

where r is the number of FoVs, N(r) is the mean number of recovered clusters, N_O corresponds to the total number of observed clusters and τ indicates how many regions must be imaged to recover most of the known cell phenotypes.

The tau parameter can be modeled as a function of the FoV width w:

where C and α are two positive constants and depend on the sample, respectively representing the study setup and tissue spatial segregation.

The Visium-based model was validated on multiple imaging mass cytometry (IMC) datasets.The results of the model on lymph node IMC data show a similar plateauing curve for every FoV size:

The study also investigated the estimation of cell type proportions as a function of sampled FoVs in a breast cancer dataset

The accuracy of the estimates was measured by Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence.

where P is the sampled proportion vector and Q is the proportion vector computed using the full dataset.

The optimal number and size of the FoVs depends on the spatial segregation of the target tissue. Heart tissue has low spatial segregation while breast cancer has high segregation.

Eskaros et al. 2017

Larger core size has superior technical and analytical accuracy in bladder tissue microarray

Larger core size increases analytical and technical accuracy in bladder cancer. Ki-67 IHC and manual mitotic spindle count. Analytical accuracy: Inter-core variability (replicate core SD) and Pearson correlation between cores and source WSI. Ordinal technical accuracy based on amount of core tissue. Large confidence intervals due to low sample size. No vTMAs. Annotated TMA sampling. Faulty??

Approach:

“Comparison of 0.6 vs. 1.0 mm TMA cores by analytical and technical accuracy of cores against WSI”.

Diagnostic slides from three tissue blocks per patient were assessed by pathologists to identify the target tissues (normal, tumor, and cis). Core placement was chosen by a pathologist for each slide in the blocks. Three cores per tissue block were extracted for the 0.6 mm TMA (9 cores total per patient) and two cores per block for the 1.0 mm TMA (6 total).

Technical accuracy was evaluated by scoring each core by how much target tissue they captured (Score 3 if target tissue > 50% of core, 2 if < 50%, 1 if no target tissue, and 0 if no tissue at all). Only cores with a score of 2 or 3 were considered usable, and were included in the analysis. Every patient with at least 2 usable cores total was included. The quality of captured tissue was defined as the mean score of all usable cores. Difference between the TMA groups was quantified by Chi-square and Fisher’s exact test.

Analytical accuracy was evaluated by IHC staining for Ki-67 and manual mitotic spindle counts. One section from each TMA group was assessed for Ki-67 positive cells using ImageJ. Accuracy was defined as the inter-core variability (replicate ore SD) and concordance (Pearson correlation) between the mean positive cells in replicate cores and the WSI. Additionally, accuracy was determined as correlation of the mean number of mitotic figures (in 10 high-power ) with the mean number of Ki-67 positive cells in the replicate cores.

Forjaz et al. 2024

Three-dimensional assessments are necessary to determine the true, spatially-resolved composition of tissues

vTMA. TMA vs WSI vs 3D. Spatial correlation. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Composition of tissues was evaluated as pixels belonging to each tissue type. vTMA error was calculated between the per-class vTMA tissue composition and the overall composition of the WSI or 3D tissue sample. Error was defined as [measurement - expected value] / expected value. “2D assessments of “representative” slides fail particularly in enumeration of rare events, such as estimation of the density of cancer or cancer precursor cells in samples known to have low neoplastic content.” Estimates of tissue composition saturate at 40-50 WSIs. “The range in heterogeneity observed in just three 3D samples was sufficient to replicate the range of inter-patient heterogeneity in tissue composition (exceed a p-value of 0.05) within the 2D cohort”. The number of TMAs required to estimate tissue composition accurately (error < 10%) varies 10-100 for WSI, and 20-700 for 3D depending on the tissue component. Random vTMA sampling. Serial (stacked) vTMAs were not random.

Approach:

The total dataset consisted of 60 TMA cores from 30 patients, WSI from 64 patients, and 3D reconstructed tissue blocks from 7 patients, plus 7 normal controls. Tissue images were segmented into 10 tissue types (pancreatic cancer, pancreatic cancer precursor lesions, normal ductal epithelium, acinar tissue, islets of Langerhans, vasculature, nerves, fat, and stroma) at a 1 µm per pixel scale using a deep learning pipeline.

Tissue composition in WSI was defined as the area percentage of each type. Variation in tissue composition was defined as the distribution of compositions of each tissue type. 3D variation in tissue composition was defined as the distribution of compositions of each tissue type, taking each serial image in the z-stack as an independent measurement.

vTMA were generated by taking random coordinates from the WSIs or 3D z-stack and taking a 1 mm diameter disk, centered on the coordinates, from the image. The tissue composition of the first virtual core was calculated and compared against the WSI and 3D compositions. Then a second core was added, and the combined sample of the cores was used in re-comparison with the WSI and 3D. This was repeated for up to 100 cores for WSI and 800 cores for 3D.

Decay in spatial correlation in the 3D samples was assessed by calculating the cross-correlations between the proportions of each tissue type between the images in the z-stack, along with recording the distance between each image. Additionally, to simulate core needle biopsies, serial (stacked) vTMAs were extracted from the 3D z-stack based on annotated selection of the initial core from the top layer, and error in composition was calculated for each serial core to the initial core.

Pancreatic neoplastic content was assessed in two forms: pancreatic cancer precursor lesions (PanIN) content was defined as the PanIN volume normalized by total volume of PanIN and normal ductal epithelium, and pancreatic cancer (PDAC) content was defined as PDAC volume normalized by total PDAC, epithelial duct and PanIN volume. For 3D evaluation, subvolumes were calculated for each combination of 90 images in the z-stack, and relative error for each was calculated in relation to the whole 3D volume.

Wilcox rank sum test was used for all significance tests. Median, mean, sd, and interquartile range were calculated for comparisons between and within cohorts. Relative error was defined as (measured value - expected value) / expected value.

Gruber et al. 2020

The Determination of Immunomodulation and Its Impact on Survival of Rectal Cancer Patients Depends on the Area Comprising a Tissue Microarray

VTMA. TIL in CRC. CD3 and CD8 IHC. Ordinal classification and expression values. Spearman’s rank corr, Kruskal-Wallis. 5-6 cores needed. Some weak correlations (CD3+ R= 0.35, 0.15; CD8+ R= 0.45, 0.69). RANDOM SAMPLING per tumor area.

Approach:

The cohort consisted of tissue sections from 60 CRC patients treated with radiation + 15 treatment-naive controls. IHC was conducted with markers for CD3 and CD8 TILs, as well as immune checkpoint proteins PD-1 and PD-L1. Tissue was annotated into categories central tumor (CT) or invasive margin (IM) by pathologists, who then manually scored marker expression in both as none (0), low (1), moderate (2) or high (3) with respect to positive cells present.

Total of 12 vTMA cores were extracted per section - 6 central and 6 marginal, of sizes 0.6 and 1.0 mm, respectively, were extracted using StrataQuest. Tumors were classified by CD3 and CD8 density high/low for immunoscore calculation. Patients were stratified into four categories 1-4 or dichotomized to 0 vs. 1-4.

Correlations between WSI and vTMA were calculated with Spearman rank correlations. Differences between groups were calculated using the Kruskal-Wallis test.

(Figure: Variance of correlations between WSI and any number of vTMA.)

Hecht et al.2008

Validation of tissue microarray technology in ovarian cancer: results from the Nurses' Health Study

Epithelial ovarian cancers. Triplicate core samples from 174 patients. No vTMAs. p53, Ki-67, ER-alpha, PR, HER-2, WT-1, CK7, CK20 IHC. Ordinal classification of expression. Intraclass correlation (ICC) and Spearman corr. Interrater reliability. ICC >= 0.91, Spearman >= 0.87. “Using the Spearman-Brown formula, we estimated that one core is sufficient to obtain a reliability of ≥ 0.75 for each antigen in all of the strata evaluated”. Borderline tumors tricky.

Approach: The cohort was a mix of 174 borderline, invasive and primary peritoneal tumors of epithelial ovarian cancer. TMA cores were extracted from tissue blocks by core needle biopsy, triplicate cores for each patient. Stained cores were manually evaluated by a pathologist and categorized by the percent of positive cells: 0, 1-10,11-25, 26-50, >50 %. Cores with missing tissue or < 20 cells were deemed non-interpretable.

The intra- and interpatient variance in patients was estimated using a random effects model and used to calculate the ICC, providing an estimate of the reproducibility across the triplicate cores. ICC was calculated as (inter-patient variance) / (inter-patient variance + intra-patient variance). Natural logarithmic transformation of the stain values was used because it was more normal.

Spearman correlation was used to see if the staining for each protein in the three cores was correlated. Mean of the coefficients for each core was used in the Spearman-Bowman formula to estimate the number of cores required to represent the entire tumor sample. Fisher’s exact test was used to assess results by histologic type and sample age. Positive staining was assessed as the maximum value of the triplicate cores, which was highly correlated with the mean and median of the cores. Cores were categorized as positive or negative at a cutoff of 10% of cells staining positive. Cohen’s kappa was used to assess inter-rater agreement.

Unconditional logistic regression was used to evaluate if sample age, invasiveness (invasive or borderline), or histology (serous [inc poorly differentiated and primary peritoneal], endometrioid, mucinous, clear cell, or other) is associated with sample non-interpretability, reported with OR and CI.

Using the Spearman-Brown formula, we estimated that one core is sufficient to obtain a reliability of ≥ 0.75 for each antigen in all of the strata evaluated (data not shown).

Hoos et al. 2001

Validation of Tissue Microarrays for Immunohistochemical Profiling of Cancer Specimens Using the Example of Human Fibroblastic Tumors

Old school validation of TMA. No vTMA. Ki-67, p53, pRB IHC. Comparison of 1-3 cores of 0.66 mm size. Ordinal (2-3) class matching only. Correlations between phenotype and outcome were not significantly different between WSI and TMA. Fibroblastic tumors. Annotated sampling.

Approach: Patient cohort consisted of 59 patients with fibroblastic neoplasms (24 desmoid, 21 low-grade fibrosarcomas, and 14 high-grade fibrosarcomas). Tissue sections and triplicate needle cores were taken from the same block. Ki-67 and p53 staining was categorized as positive/negative. A match between WSI and cores was defined as having at least two cores in agreement with the WSI. Agreement between WSI and cores was 96% for Ki-67 and 98% for p53, respectively. pRB was categorized as high, moderate, or negative, with 91% agreement.

Karlsson et al. 2009

Tissue Microarray Validation: A Methodologic Study with Special Reference to Lung Cancer

vTMA. Validation of TMA in Ki-67 positivity proportion in lung cancer, ordinal classification of p53 positivity in lung tumors, ordinal classif. of Ki-67, HER-2 and estrogen receptor in breast and endometrial carcinomas. Comparison of size 0.6 vs 1.0 mm cores, with little difference in results (ICC 0.96). Supports a hot-spot approach. Intraclass correlation as error measure. Samples based on pathologist’s annotations.

Approach: Patient cohorts included 29 cases of lung carcinoma (small cell, squamous cell, adenocarcinoma) for Ki-67 and p53 expression analysis, 10 cases of endometrial and breast carcinomas for ER, Ki-67, and HER-2 scoring. On the lung cancer specimen, Ki-67 expression was manually counted as the percentage of positive cells. p53 expression positivity was categorized into classes < 10 %, 10 - 30 %, > 30 %. For the breast and endometrial samples, Ki-67 expression was stratified as < 10 %, 10 - 20 %, > 20 %, while ER was < 10 %, >= 10 %. Hercep (HER-2?) was scored on a 0-3 scale.

Tumor area on H&E slides was annotated by a pathologist and transferred to IHC slides.In this area, five spots were picked representing central and border areas. The spots were imaged from the center to the periphery until areas corresponding to cylinders of 0.6 mm and 1.0 mm were captured.

Agreement between 0.6 mm and 1.0 mm scores was measured by intraclass correlation (ICC) and Bland-Altman plots. Coefficient of variation estimated by within-subject SD / mean value. SEM = within-subject SD / sqrt(n cores. 95% CI = 2* SEM

Figure 1. Bland-Altman plot showing the mean difference in the proportion of Ki-67 positive cells between the observations made using tissue cylinders of 0.6 and 1.0 mm diameters. The difference is 0.5%; the limits of agreement are also shown (8.9 and -7.9). Data are based on the entire data set. ADCA, adenocarcinoma; SCC, squamous cell carcinoma; SCLC, small cell lung carcinoma.

Figure 2. Nomogram as a guideline for choosing the number of cylinders to achieve a precision determined by the length of an approximate 95% confidence interval for the mean, 2 SD/sqrt(c). (e.g., For a precision criterion of 8 units, the dotted horizontal line from 8 intersects the polygon for ADCA between two and three cylinders and for ‘All’ between three and four cylinders. These values will be sufficient for this precision criterion).

Khan & Yuan 2016

Biopsy variability of lymphocytic infiltration in breast cancer subtypes and the ImmunoSkew score

vTMA: Measures TIL in breast cancer. Lymphocyte proportion estimate accuracy plateaus at 15 cores. Little improvement in ordinal classification after 4 cores. 0.6 mm cores. Significant differences between subtypes; HER2+ least correlated. Inter-tumour heterogeneity in TIL burden of LMS greatly outweighs intra-tumour heterogeneity. Tissue sections from the top, middle and bottom of a block were used. Pearson correlation, Kruskal-Wallis, two-sided paired t-test, AUC. Random TMA sampling.

Approach: The discovery cohort consisted of treatment-naive breast tumor tissue blocks from 484 patients. Three sections were taken from each block, one each from the top, middle and bottom of the block, and placed on the same imaging slide. The slides were H&E stained and imaged. PAM50 status was determined by expression microarrays. Tumor cellularity was categorized into low, moderate, or high (< 40 %, 40 > < 70 %, > 70 % tumor DNA). Lymphocytic infiltration was similarly classed as absent, mild or severe by pathologists.

Slide contents were segmented algorithmically. vTMA were constructed by picking the first cell in a list to be a potential core center, checking if there are more than T = 100 cells within the core radius (R=0.3 mm), and then either accepting or rejecting the core. If the core is accepted, remove all enclosed cells from the list of cells. If not, remove the proposed center from the list. On average, 88.53 cores were generated for each sample.

Tumor-based lymphocyte ratio was calculated as the number of lymphocytes divided by the total number of cells present in all sections from a single tumor. Core-based lymphocyte ratio was determined as the number of lymphocytes divided by the total number of cells present in a vTMA core. The ImmunoSkew score was determined as the skewness of the distribution of core-based lymphocyte ratio in a tumour as a measure of spatial heterogeneity in lymphocytic infiltrate. Mathematically,

Core-based scores were compared with tumor-based scores by randomly sampling n cores and benchmarking their scores against tumor-based scores. This was repeated 100 times for each n = 1…50 per tumor. For samples with less than 50 cores, sampling with replacement was used. For survival analysis, n cores were randomly sampled from each tumor and the average lymphocyte ratio score was evaluated. This was repeated 50 times for each n = 1…50. Only samples with 50+ cores were used.

Agreement between continuous variables was assessed using Pearson correlation. Between continuous and categorical variables, Kruskal-Wallis was used. Two-sided paired t-test was used to assess concordance between matched continuous variables. The accuracy of core-based lymphocyte ratio as a predictor of immune abundance was estimated by AUC, with random sampling to estimate 95% CI. Kaplan-meier was used for univariate survival analysis, with p-values calculated using log-rank test.Continuous measures, like the ImmunoSkew, were dichotomised by evaluating every hundredth value of said measure in the 20-80 % range using univariate analysis. Values best dichotomising survival in the discovery cohort were tested as cut-off values against the discovery cohort. Cox proportional hazard model was fitted to the survival data to compute hazard ratios and 95% CI to determine the prognostic value. Cox was also used for multivariate analysis for predictors found significant by univariate analysis.

Koyuncu et al. 2023

Visual Assessment of 2-Dimensional Levels Within 3-Dimensional Pathology Data Sets of Prostate Needle Biopsies Reveals Substantial Spatial Heterogeneity

Intrabiopsy variability in prostate cancer with open-top light-sheet microscopy (OTLS). No vTMAs.

Approach: 20 cases of prostate cancer were randomly picked from a dataset of 200. One cancerous biopsy per case was included in the study. Each biopsy core was 3D imaged using OTLS microscopy. 5 cross-section images were taken from each core at 100 μm intervals, for a total of 100 images. Each image was given a Gleason Grade by 4 pathologists.

Intrabiopsy variability was assessed as the worst pairwise agreement between two imaged levels in a core, measured by Cohen kappa. Each image level was considered an independent observation.

Le Page et al. 2021

Using a convolutional neural network for classification of squamous and non‐squamous non‐small cell lung cancer based on diagnostic histopathology HES images

vTMA. Neural network to classify tissue as squamous or non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer. Can use annotated WSI or vTMA for diagnosis.

Lee et al. 2019

The adequacy of tissue microarrays in the assessment of inter- and intra-tumoural heterogeneity of infiltrating lymphocyte burden in leiomyosarcoma

vTMA. Studies TIL burden in leiomyosarcoma (LMS). Most types have low and homogenous burden, with some exceptional subtypes. 1 mm cores. Ordinal classification can use <3 cores, but count/proportional estimates need 11+ cores. CD3, CD4, CD8 and CD20 IHC. Mean of 20 cores taken as the “true” value. “how many cores needed to be sampled in order for >80% of possible combinations to produce an estimated TIL burden that fell within each of three different thresholds: (i) +/−20% of true TIL burden, (ii) same side of cohort median or (iii) in same cohort quartile as true TIL burden across the entire cohort.” Difference in TIL levels between central tumor and periphery. Inter-tumour heterogeneity in TIL burden of LMS greatly outweighs intra-tumour heterogeneity. Annotated and random sampling.

Approach: The cohort of 47 primary LMS cases was annotated and verified by pathologists. 5 FFPE tissue blocks per sample were retrieved, originating from spatially distinct tumor areas (at least two marginal and central). IHC staining for CD3, CD4 and CD8 was conducted on three consecutive tissue sections, respectively. IHC images were scored by manually counting the number of positive cells for each marker in ten non-adjacent tumor-containing high-power fields.

For assessing the optimal number of cores, vTMA were constructed by picking 20 random 1.0 mm diameter circles from viable tumor areas in H&E images. For assessing intra-tumoral variance, 20 cores were picked from the marginal (within 3 mm of resection margin) and central (< 10 mm from nearest margin) tumor areas. Corresponding areas were then selected on the IHC slides. Images of the areas at x10 magnification were exported as .tif files and cropped to 0.785 mm^2 in Image J. TIL expression was counted usin the ‘particle analysis’ function of Image J after optimising pixel intensity, particle size and circularity thresholds. Bland-Altman analysis found the configuration to be associated with a bias of -0.52 cells (95% limits of agreement -17, +16). This approach couldn’t be used for CD4 because of pleomorphic histiocytes.

Physical TMA cores were extracted in triplicate from viable tumor and re-embedded in a paraffin block. Consecutive sections from this TMA block were stained for H&E, CD3 and CD8. All TILs were manually counted under a brightfield microscope, with values expressed as the average of the triplicate cores.

TIL burden was assessed as average number of CD3+, CD4+ or CD8+ TIL per HPF (High-power field) from 50 HPF per tumor (10 per tumor block). Comparison of TIL burden of tumors from different anatomical sites of origin and grade was assessed using 1-way ANOVA of Log2-transformed average TIL/HPF values using Prism v7.0. To assess inter- vs. intra-tumoral variance in TIL, the relative magnitudes of block-effect and tumor-effect were counted from the total variance by Log2 transformation of all raw TIL/HPF values. 95% CI of TIL/HPF was calculated for each tumor block, and across all 5 tumor blocks. The numbers of positive and total neoplastic cells counted in each image were added in descending order of the ratio of positive cells to total cells until the total number of neoplastic cells exceeded 999. If the 3 microarray samples did not contain more than 999 neoplastic cells, all the neoplastic cells in the 3 microarray samples were counted to determine the KI or AI. The first two measurements were used to assess intrarater reliability by investigating the intraclass correlation coefficients for single measurements and the averages of two measurements. The mean values were used for Bland-Altman plot analysis and Kendall tau.The Bland-Altman plot was used to compare the two measurement methods. An ordinary least-squares analysis was performed on the plot. A Fixed bias was indicated to exist if the 95% CI for the mean value of the difference did not contain 0. A proportional bias was indicated to exist if the slope of the regression of the differences in the means differed significantly from 0. Kendall tau was used to assess the correlations between KI and AI in the WSI and vTMA.

In the Bland–Altman plot analysis, differences between the KI and AI measurements in the TMA samples and whole mount tissue preparations were nearly all within the limits of agreement or the 95% CIs of these limits. However, we found fixed biases in the KI measurements and fixed and proportional biases in the AI measurements. A smaller TMA sample diameter was correlated with a larger systematic bias.[32] Our findings clearly demonstrated that these indices differed when they were measured using virtual TMA samples or whole mount tissue preparations. Although ordinary least-squares regression analysis is used in Bland–Altman plots to estimate the slope, caution is required when evaluating the fixed bias if a proportional bias exists. If a proportional bias exists (slope ≠ 0) and the mean difference almost inevitably deviates from zero, there is a risk that fixed bias will be overestimated.[30] If a proportional bias exists in 1 direction (e.g., slope > 0) and a fixed bias exists in the opposite direction, there is a risk that the fixed bias will be underestimated.

Multiple tissue types with variable spatial segregation. TMA sampling strategies wrt. core number and size. Higher core number beneficial for high seg tissues. KL-divergence as a measure of error. Random TMA sampling.

Eskaros et al. 2017: Larger core size increases analytical and technical accuracy in bladder cancer. Ki-67 IHC and manual mitotic spindle count. Analytical accuracy: Inter-core variability (replicate core SD) and Pearson correlation between cores and source WSI. Ordinal technical accuracy based on amount of core tissue. Large confidence intervals due to low sample size. No vTMAs. Annotated TMA sampling. Faulty??

Forjaz et al. 2024. vTMA. TMA vs WSI vs 3D. Spatial correlation. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC). Composition of tissues was evaluated as pixels belonging to each tissue type. vTMA error was calculated between the per-class vTMA tissue composition and the overall composition of the WSI or 3D tissue sample. Error was defined as [measurement - expected value] / expected value. “2D assessments of “representative” slides fail particularly in enumeration of rare events, such as estimation of the density of cancer or cancer precursor cells in samples known to have low neoplastic content.” Estimates of tissue composition saturate at 40-50 WSIs. “The range in heterogeneity observed in just three 3D samples was sufficient to replicate the range of inter-patient heterogeneity in tissue composition (exceed a p-value of 0.05) within the 2D cohort”. The number of TMAs required to estimate tissue composition accurately (error < 10%) varies 10-100 for WSI, and 20-700 for 3D depending on the tissue component. Random vTMA sampling. Serial (stacked) vTMAs were not random.

Gruber et al. 2020. VTMA. TIL in CRC. CD3 and CD8 IHC. Ordinal classification and expression values. Spearman’s rank corr, Kruskal-Wallis. 5-6 cores needed. Some weak correlations. RANDOM SAMPLING per tumor area.

Hecht et al. 2018: Epithelial ovarian cancers. Triplicate core samples from 174 patients. No vTMAs. p53, Ki-67, ER-alpha, PR, HER-2, WT-1, CK7, CK20 IHC. Ordinal classification of expression. Intraclass correlation (ICC) and Spearman corr. Interrater reliability. ICC >= 0.91, Spearman >= 0.87. “Using the Spearman-Brown formula, we estimated that one core is sufficient to obtain a reliability of ≥ 0.75 for each antigen in all of the strata evaluated (data not shown)”. Borderline tumors tricky.

Hoos et al. 2001: Old school validation of TMA. Ki-67, p53, pRB IHC. Comparison of 1-3 cores. Ordinal class matching only. Fibroblastic tumors. Annotated sampling.

Karlsson et al. 2009: vTMA. Validation of TMA in Ki-67 positivity proportion in lung cancer, ordinal classification of p53 positivity in lung tumors, ordinal classif. of Ki-67 and estrogen receptor in breast and endometrial carcinomas. Comparison of 0.6 vs 1.0 mm cores, with little difference in results.Supports a hot-spot approach. Intraclass correlation as error measure. Samples based on pathologist’s annotations.

Khan & Yuan 2016: vTMA: Measures TIL in breast cancer. Lymphocyte proportion estimate accuracy plateaus at 15 cores. Little improvement in ordinal classification after 4 cores. 0.6 mm cores. Significant differences between subtypes; HER2+ least correlated. Inter-tumour heterogeneity in TIL burden of LMS greatly outweighs intra-tumour heterogeneity. Tissue sections from the top, middle and bottom of a block were used. Pearson correlation, Kruskal-Wallis, two-sided paired t-test, AUC. Random TMA sampling.

Koyuncu et al. 2023: Intrabiopsy variability in prostate cancer with open-top light-sheet microscopy (OTLS). No vTMAs.

Le Page et al. 2021: vTMA. Neural network to classify tissue as squamous or non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer. Can use annotated WSI or vTMA for diagnosis.

Lee et al. 2019: vTMA. Studies TIL burden in leiomyosarcoma (LMS). Most types have low and homogenous burden, with some exceptional subtypes. 1 mm cores. Ordinal classification can use <3 cores, but count/proportional estimates need 11+ cores. CD3, CD4, CD8 and CD20 IHC. Mean of 20 cores taken as the “true” value. “how many cores needed to be sampled in order for >80% of possible combinations to produce an estimated TIL burden that fell within each of three different thresholds: (i) +/−20% of true TIL burden, (ii) same side of cohort median or (iii) in same cohort quartile as true TIL burden across the entire cohort.” Difference in TIL levels between central tumor and periphery. Inter-tumour heterogeneity in TIL burden of LMS greatly outweighs intra-tumour heterogeneity. Annotated and random sampling.

Lin et al. 2023: vTMA. TMA vs WSI vs 3D. Cell type proportions and neighborhoods. Spatial correlation of TMA leads to low effective sample size in cores. 1 mm cores. Cytokeratin, Ki-67, CD3, CD20, CD45RO, CD4 CD8a, CD68 CD163, FOXP3, PD1, PDL1, CD31, a-SMA, desmin, and CD45 IHC. Inter-core variance >> inter-patient variability for all biomarkers, except for Ki-67 pos in CK-cells. Large-scale molecular gradients associated with morphological gradients. 2D methods lose 3D context. Inter-slide error < inter-patient error in WSI vs 3D. Errors from variance. Colorectal cancer. Some immune stuff. Annotated sampling.

Liudahl et al. 2021: vTMA. Investigates TIL density, complexity, spatial relationships, functionality and differentiation in PDAC. 10+ cores needed to get 75% probability of being within 20% of the true mean abundance. True mean defined as mean of all cores (19-28). 1 mm cores. Mann-Whitney, Kruskal-Wallis, mixed-effect models, multiple t-tests, Spearman rank. High myeloid inflammation results in a worse disease outcome (SS). Annotated sampling.

Lovane et al. 2024: vTMA. Validation of TMA in cervical cancer using vTMA. IHC of p16, CK5, CK7, PD-L1, CD8. vTMA cores of 0.6 mm size. Ordinal scoring. Hot-spot based estimates deteriorate with increasing cores. Error as kappa-stats. Annotated sampling with randomized combinations. Lots of references.

Mi et al. 2021: vTMA. Model can predict Triple-negative breast cancer response to treatment. TMA validation against vTMA???. 0.6mm vTMA cores. Pearson corr. Graph representation of cells. Sampling unknown.

Nielsen et al. 2023: vTMA: Standardization of Ki-67 assessment in breast cancer, to find a biomarker for avoiding radiotherapy. stainAnalyzer software randomly selects 5 virtual cores (1 mm) and iteratively adds more based on confidence in analysis results. Cores are annotated by 2-3 pathologists. Kappa stats.

Nirmal et al. 2022: RCNs.

Oshiro et al. 2016: vTMA. Systematic bias in TMAs from bladder cancer. Ki-67 index (KI) and apoptotic index (AI) only. Semi-random sampling. “For each whole mount tissue preparation, we used a random number table to randomly select 3 small, round areas in which at least one third of the area contained neoplastic cells.” 0.6 and 1 mm cores. Bland-Altman, Kendall tau.

Pedersen et al. 2014: vTMA. Validation of TMA in peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL) using vTMA. 1 mm cores. CD2, CD30, Ki-67 IHC. 6 cores were within 10% of WSI. 3-4 cores needed for 70% agreement with 6 cores. Error measured by Bland-Altman plots provided with estimates of mean difference and 95% limits of agreement (LIM). Large confidence intervals. Random sampling from representative tumor areas.

Quintayuo et al. 2014: vTMA. Validation of TMA in ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) using vTMA. IHC of ER, PgR, HER2 and Ki-67. 0.6 mm cores. Positivity % for Ki-67, histoscore for others. Only 2 cores needed. Interclass correlation coefficient, Pearson correlation. Annotated sampling.

Ramanathan et al. 2014: vTMA. Validation of TMA in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC). p53 IHC. Tested 2-6 cores of sizes 0.6, 1.0, 1.5 mm. 3 cores optimal. Annotated sampling. H-score with one-sample t-tests and intra-class correlation.High correlation for all. Contains references to prev vtma.

Sobottka et al. 2024: vTMA. TIL in clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). Annotated sampling only. NO VALIDATION

Wampfler et al. 2011: vTMA. Validation of TMA in Non-small cell lung cancer using vTMA. IHC of TTF-1, p53, Ki-67. Positive % for all markers. Accuracy measured by exact or range matches in 95% CI, and precision as the width of CI. Sufficient accuracy when lower bound of 95% CI > 0. Three cores optimal for TFF-1 and p53, with inconclusive results for Ki-67. The latter two tend to underestimate the true value. Annotated sampling.

Weis et al. 2018: vTMA. TMA vs WSI. Pan-cytokeratin IHC. Prognosis is based on tumor budding hotspots. TMA can't find hotspots. Very low correlation on vTMA results. Colorectal cancer (CRC). Random sampling.

Wilson et al. 2021: vTMA. Post-mortem neuropathological study. Only 1 core needed on TDP-43 and 1-2 cores on microglia. Unspecified sampling.
